text: The Cooper test distance is the strongest single predictor of half-marathon race time among field and laboratory variables, with a correlation of r = -0.93.

Half-marathon race time can be predicted from Cooper test distance using the equation: Race time (min) = 201.26 - 0.03433 × Cooper distance (m), explaining 87.3% of variance with a standard error of 3.78 minutes.
A laboratory model using velocity at VO2max and body weight predicts half-marathon time with R² = 0.77 and SEE of 5.28 minutes, which is less accurate than the Cooper test model.
Velocity at VO2max (vVO2max) correlates strongly with half-marathon race time (r = -0.84), making it the best laboratory predictor of half-marathon performance.
Body weight has a significant positive correlation with half-marathon race time (r = 0.40), indicating heavier recreational runners tend to run slower half-marathons.
A simple field test (Cooper 12-minute run) provides better half-marathon performance prediction than laboratory-based physiological testing including VO2max and ventilatory thresholds.

Oxygen consumption at the second ventilatory threshold (VO2VT2) correlates strongly with half-marathon race time (r = -0.79), confirming the importance of threshold capacity for half-marathon performance.
